5. Linear Algebra in a Ring

5.1. Modules

The analog for a ring for a vector space over a field is called a module.

Definition 29. Let be a ring. An -module is an abelian group with addition and scalar multiple defined such that for all and

These are the same as the axioms for a vector space (for a more comprehensive definition, see Definition 54). The fact that a ring lacks multiplicative invertibility makes modules more complicated. For example, not every module has a basis.

Note. For finite rings, direct sum and direct product are the same thing. It does not hold for infinite.

Examples.

  1. Let be an ideal. Then and are -modules.
  3. Free module of -vectors, column vectors with entries in .
  4. is a -module.
  5. Let be the right of polynomials over a field . Let be an -module. Then, can be seen as an -vector space and scalar multiplication by is a linear operator on . More generally, we have the following proposition:
Proposition 32. modules correspond to -vector spaces with a chosen linear operator.

This is subtle. We have two notions of modulues here. If generate , i.e. , we also have generates an -vector space , which with the linear operator defines

Proposition 33. Abelian group and -module are equivalent concepts.
Proof

If we start with a -module, we can forget about scalar multiplication to get an abelian group under addition. Starting with an abelian group under addition, we can define scalar multiplication as repeated addition to get a -module.

□

Definition 30. Let be an -module.

  1. A submodule of an module is a nonempty subset that is closed under addition and scalar multiplication.
  2. A homomorpshim between two -modules is a map compatible with the laws of composition. The kernel is a submodule of the domain, and the image is a submodule of the range.

(39)

for and .

  1. Let be a submodule. The quotient module is the group of additive cosets . It is made into an -module by the rule .

For example, the submodules of the free module are the ideals of . This pretty much comes from the definition.

Theorem 19 (Main facts about quotient modules). Let be a submodule of an -module .

  1. The set of additive cosets of in is an -module, and the canonical map sending is a surjective homomorphism of -modules whose kernel is .
  2. Mapping property:Let be a homomorphism of -modules whose kernel contains . There is a unique homomorphism such that .
  3. First Isomorphism Theorem: Let be a surjective homomorphism of -modules whose kernel is equal to . The map defined in the mapping property is an isomorphism.
  4. Correspondence Theorem: Let be a surjective homomorphism of -modules with kernel . There is a bijective correspondence between the submodules of and submodules of that contain . This correspondence is defined as follosw: . [TODO] If and are corresponding modules, then .
Proof

Follow proof for analogous facts for rings/ideals and groups/normal subgroups.

□

The main facts we will study in this chapter are

  1. Classification of finitely generated abelian groups
  2. Hilbert basis theorem

5.2. Free Modules

Definition 31. A basis for an -module is a set that generates and is linearly independent with respect to linear combinations over .

Examples:

  1. Any finite-dimensional complex vector spaces has a basis.
  2. as a module over is generated by but . It does not have a basis.
  3. If generates and , then we have a surjective homomorphism with .

Definition 32.

  1. An -module is a free module if .
  2. A -module is a free abelian group if .
Proposition 34. A module is free if and only if it has a basis.
Proof

Follows immediately from the definition of a basis.

□

Remarks.

  1. Suppose -modules and have bases. Then any homomorphism is representable by a matrix with coefficients in .

Proposition 35.

  1. The change of basis matrix in a free module is an invertible -matrix.
  2. Any two bases of the same free module over have the same cardinality.
Proof

□

5.3. Diagonalizing integer matrices

When solving linear systems, we can perform row/column operations, expressable as multiplication by invertible square matrices on the left/right. The row operations are listed below. The operations for columns are the same.

  1. Swap rows
  2. Scalar multiplication of rows by units ()
  3. Add scalar multiplication of 1 row to another row.
Proposition 36. is invertible in if and only if is a unit in that ring.
Proof

Forward proof: Since the determinant is multiplicative, we have , implying is a unit.
Backward proof: If is a unit, the classical adjugate formula explicitly constructs an inverse.

□

We previously showed in linear algebra over a field that any matrix is similar to a rectangular diagonal matrix containing the identity matrix. The theorem is included below

Theorem 20 (Artin 4.2.10). Let be a linear transformation between f.d. vector spaces. There are bases and of and , respectively, such that the matrix of with respect to these bases has the form.

  1. In other words, given an matrix , there are invertible matrices and such that has the form shown above.
Proof

Consider expanding to a basis in and to a basis in . We can convert a square matrix to the identity matrix using the fact that a basis of is mapped to a basis of .

□

Theorem 21 (Smith Normal Form). Let be an integer matrix. There exist products and of elementary integer matrices of appropriate sizes, so that is diagonal, where the diagonal entries are positive and each one divides the next.
Proof

By permuting rows and columns, move the smallest nonzero entry (multplying be −1 if necessary) to the upper left entry . Clear out the column using division, possibly requiring us to reshift the top left entry. Do something similar to clear out the first row. Now we are left with a matrix in the form

(40)

Assume some entry of is not divisible by . We can perform a similar division with remainder step and return to step 1.

□

Practically, we compute the Smith Normal Form using row operations to get the gcd of all the entries in the top left corner and going from there.

The integer solutions of the equation are the integer vectors whose first coordinates are zero. This implies the solutions to are of the form .

5.4. Noetherian Rings

Definition 33.

  1. An -module is finitely generated if there exists a finite set such that any can be expressed as a linearly combination in .
  2. is finitely-presented if …

Motivation: A finitely-generated module is not necessarily finitely-presented. We want to classify the types of rings whose modules imply this.

Definition 34. A ring is Noetherian if every ideal of is finitely generated.

Examples:

  • Any PID, e.g. and , is Noetherian since every ideal is generated by one element (PID property).
  • turns out to be Noetherian by the Hilbert basis theorem.
  • is not Noetherian. Consider the ideal of polynomials with no constant term.
Lemma 29 (Ascending Chain Condition). Let be a Noetherian ring. Then any increasing set of ideals stabilizes, i.e. there exists such that . The converse also holds.
Proof

Obvious.

□

Corollary. Every proper ideal of a Noetherian ring is contained in a maximal ideal.

In general submodules of finitely generated modules need not be finitely generated. As an example, consider the ring . itself is finitely generated by but the submodule of polynomials with zero constant term is not finitely generated. Another example, consider as a submodule of its fraction field .

Theorem 22 (Submodules are finitely generated). Let be a Noetherian ring. Every submodule of a finitely generated -module is finitely generated.
Proof

□

We know attempt to prove Hilbert’s basis theorem.

Lemma 30. Let be a homomorphism.

  1. If is finitely generated then is also finitely generated.

    • is not necessarily finitely generated!
  2. If and are finitely generated, then is also finitely generated (rank-nullity).
Proof
  1. Obvious given that is surjective.
  2. Suppose is finitely generated by . Choose vectors in the preimage that map to accordingly. Then the union of this set plus the finite set that generates generates . Proof: Suppose . Take any preimage of . It’s difference with belongs in . Hcen .

□

Proposition 37 (Quotient is Noetherian). Let be a Noetherian ring, and let be an ideal of . The quotient ring is Noetherian.

More generally, uppose is surjective. If is Noetherian then so is .

Proof

Consider Lemma 30 on the canonical map .

□

Corollary. Let be a polynomial ring in a finite number of variables over the integers or over a field. Any ring that is isomorphic to a quotient ring is Noetherian.

Theorem 23 (Hilbert Basis Theorem). Let be a Noetherian ring. The polynomial ring is Noetherian.
Proof

[TODO]

□

Corollary 1. By induction, if is Noetherian, then is Noetherian.

Important in algebraic geometry.

Fun fact. If is Noetherian. Not all of its subrings are Noetherian. Counterexample: Take a non-Noetherian ring like and let be the fraction field of it.

We will use the fact that is abelian to classify finitely generated abelian groups.

Proposition 39 (Changing generators and relations). Let be an presentation matrix for an -module . Then the following matrices present the same module .

  1. with
  2. with
  3. obtained by deleting a column of zeros.
  4. The -th column of is and is obtained by deleting the th row and th column of .
Proof

(1) and (2) correspond to change of basis. (3) corresponds to a trivial relation. (4) corresponds to , so we can remove it from the generating set.

□

5.6. Classification of finitely generated abelian groups

Theorem 24. Let be a finitely generated abelian group. Then is isomorphic to the direct sum of cyclic groups. But more stringently, we have the isomorphism classes are where . We can ignore since its a trivial group.
Proof

We can do something similar with modules in PIDs as a isomorphic as the sum of cyclic modules ()

Definition 36. A cyclic -module is a module that is generated by a single element.

For example, a principal ideal in is a cyclic -module.

Theorem 25 (Structure theorem for F[t]-modules). Every finitely generated module is a product of cyclic modules.
Proof
